segments.paradiso-9-g14: Li is the plural definite article (archaic form of modern 'i' or 'gli'); 'occhi' is the plural noun 'eyes'; 'di' is the preposition linking the noun to the name; 'Bëatrice' is the proper name (Beatrice). Together this nominative phrase names the subject ('the eyes of Beatrice').
segments.paradiso-9-g15: Ch’ is the clitic form of the relative pronoun ('che' = 'who/which') introducing a relative clause; 'eran' is the imperfect indicative of 'essere' (they were); 'fermi' is an adjective (plural) meaning 'fixed/steady'; 'sovra me' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'above me' or 'over me' indicating gaze direction.
segments.paradiso-9-g16: 'come' is a comparative/linking adverb meaning 'as' or 'just as'; 'pria' (archaic) means 'before' or 'previously' — together 'come pria' = 'as before' and links back to an earlier state.
segments.paradiso-9-g17: 'di' introduces a noun phrase ('di caro assenso' = 'of dear/precious assent'); 'caro' is an adjective meaning 'dear/precious'; 'assenso' is a noun 'assent/consent'; 'al' = 'a + il' ('to the'); 'mio disio' = 'my desire' (possessive + noun); 'certificato' is a past participle used adjectivally here ('certified/assured'); 'fermi' repeats the adjective 'steady' agreeing in number with 'occhi' — the whole phrase says their steady state was certificato (assured) by a dear assent to the speaker's desire.
segments.paradiso-9-g18: ‘Deh’ is an interjection (archaic/poetic) expressing entreaty — equivalent to 'ah' or 'please' in English, used to address the spirit that follows.
segments.paradiso-9-g19: 'metti' is the 2nd person singular imperative of 'mettere' (command/request: 'put/assign/grant'); 'al' = 'a + il' ('to the'); 'mio voler' = 'my will/desire' (voler as a noun); 'tosto' is an adverb here meaning 'quickly/at once'; 'compenso' is a noun 'compensation/recompense/reward' — the clause is an imperative plea: 'grant at once a recompense to my will.'
segments.paradiso-9-g20: 'beato spirto' is a vocative addressing the addressee: 'blessed spirit' (beato = 'blessed', spirto = 'spirit/soul'); the comma and closing quote show the end of the direct address before the narrator's tag.
segments.paradiso-9-g21: 'dissi' is the 1st person singular perfect (past) of 'dire' — 'I said' — acting as the narrator's connective tag between the two quoted clauses.
segments.paradiso-9-g22: 'e' = 'and' linking clauses; 'fammi' is the imperative of 'fare' with enclitic 'mi' ('do/make for me' = 'give me' or 'let me have'); 'prova' is a noun here 'proof/test' ('fammi prova' = 'let me have proof' or 'put me to the test'); 'ch’' = 'che' introduces a subordinate clause ('that'); 'i’' (i with apostrophe) = 'io' (I); 'possa' is the present subjunctive of 'potere' expressing ability/possibility ('that I may be able'); 'in te' = 'in you' (within you/through you); 'refletter' (archaic infinitive of 'riflettere') means 'to reflect' in the sense of mirroring; 'quel ch’io penso' = 'that which I think' (quel = 'that', ch’ = relative 'which', 'io penso' = 'I think'). The whole clause requests the chance to see whether the speaker's thought can be mirrored in the spirit.
segments.paradiso-9-g23: This segment is a single period marking the end of the preceding lines; it has no lexical content to analyze.
segments.paradiso-9-g24: Onde here introduces a consequence/continuation (hence/so). The relative clause 'che m’era ancor nova' uses the imperfect era plus the enclitic m’ (mi) to mean 'which was still new to me' — era is descriptive past (imperfect) rather than a simple past.
segments.paradiso-9-g25: 'del suo profondo' is a prepositional phrase modifying the preceding clause: del = di + il 'of the/from the', suo = 'its', profondo = 'depth(s)' (here plural/depths in sense). The comma shows an inserted adverbial specification of origin.
segments.paradiso-9-g26: Ond' is the elided form of 'onde' meaning 'whence/from which'; followed by 'ella' (it/she) referring back to the light. 'Pria cantava' uses the imperfect cantava to indicate an action habitual or prior in time: 'which had sung before.'
segments.paradiso-9-g27: Seguette is passato remoto (or perfect narrative) meaning 'followed/continued.' The simile 'come a cui di ben far giova' literally 'as to whom of good-doing it is useful' is idiomatic: 'as to one for whom doing good is beneficial.' Note the infinitive far and present giova which expresses a general truth.
segments.paradiso-9-g28: This is a locative clause introduced by In quella parte...: 'In that part of the (terra) ... italica.' 'Terra prava' is an archaic phrase meaning a perverse/wicked land (adjective placed after noun, as is common in poetic syntax). 'Che siede tra...' is a relative clause: siede is present 'sits/resides' indicating location.
segments.paradiso-9-g29: Si leva un colle is an impersonal/reflective construction meaning 'a hill lifts itself up' or more simply 'a hill rises.' Si + leva yields a natural intransitive rise rather than an active raising.
segments.paradiso-9-g30: The negative clause 'e non surge molt’ alto' uses surge (present infinitive/poetic present) meaning 'rises' and the adverb molt’ (elided final vowel) + alto 'very high' — the word order is standard: not very high.
segments.paradiso-9-g31: Là onde scese già una facella is a locative + relative clause: 'therefrom (là onde) already descended a facella.' Scese is passato remoto 'descended'; già here means 'once/formerly' as well as 'already.' The following relative clause 'che fece a la contrada un grande assalto' uses fece (passato remoto of fare) 'made' and a prepositional object 'a la contrada' = 'on/against the district.'
segments.paradiso-9-g1: Da poi che forms a temporal conjunction meaning 'since/after that'. Word order is preposition + adverb + conjunction followed by the noun phrase 'Carlo tuo' ('your Charles'). 'Da' here functions like 'since' (origin/time), and 'poi che' emphasizes the subsequent time relation.
segments.paradiso-9-g2: 'bella Clemenza' is a vocative or appositive addressing an abstract personified quality; adjective + noun, 'bella' qualifying 'Clemenza' (Clemency). In poetry the epithet follows the name for emphasis.
segments.paradiso-9-g3: m'ebbe chiarito is a compound of the clitic object 'm'' (to me) + passato remoto 'ebbe' (3rd sing. of avere) + past participle 'chiarito' — literally 'had clarified to me'. The order shows the enclitic pronoun attached to the auxiliary.
segments.paradiso-9-g4: mi narrò li ’nganni che ricever dovea la sua semenza is main clause + relative. 'mi' (to me) is indirect object; 'narrò' is passato remoto 'he told/related'; 'li ’nganni' is the direct object ('the deceits'). The subordinate 'che ricever dovea la sua semenza' uses the infinitive-stem 'ricever' with the imperfect modal 'dovea' ('was to/ought to receive'), so 'that his seed was to receive.'
segments.paradiso-9-g5: 'ma disse' is a simple clause: adversative 'ma' ('but') + passato remoto 'disse' ('he said'). The colon/quotation follows to introduce direct speech.
segments.paradiso-9-g6: «Taci e lascia muover li anni» contains two imperatives: 'Taci' (be silent) and 'lascia' (let/leave), coordinated by 'e'. 'muover' is the infinitive 'to move' (here 'let [them] move' = 'let the years run their course'); 'li anni' is the direct object 'the years'.
segments.paradiso-9-g7: sì ch’io non posso dir se non che... is a result/consequence clause: 'sì che' = 'so that', followed by 'io non posso dir' (I cannot say). 'se non che' = 'except that' introduces the sole thing permitted to be said. 'pianto giusto verrà di retro ai vostri danni' places the future 'verrà' after adjectives: 'a just lamentation will come after your losses.' 'di retro' = 'behind/after'.
segments.paradiso-9-g8: This whole segment is one clause: 'E già' ('and already') introduces 'la vita di quel lume santo' (the life of that holy light) as subject. 'rivolta s'era' is a reflexive/periphrastic pluperfect (past participle 'rivolta' + auxiliary 'era') meaning 'had turned itself'; 'al Sol' = 'to the Sun', and the relative 'che la rïempie' ('that fills it') modifies 'Sol'. The simile 'come quel ben...' follows to compare the relation.
segments.paradiso-9-g9: 'Ahi' is an interjection ('alas'). 'anime ingannate' is a noun + past participle meaning 'deluded/deceived souls'. 'e fatture empie' links another noun phrase—'fatture' = 'works/creations/devices' and 'empie' is intended as 'impious/wicked' (an archaic adjective). The phrase addresses those souls directly.
segments.paradiso-9-g10: 'che' introduces a relative clause referring back to the addressees. 'da sì fatto ben' is 'from such a good' (literally 'from so made good' — 'sì fatto' is poetic for 'such'). 'torcete i cuori' uses the present indicative 2nd pl. 'torcete' = 'you twist' (you turn aside) with 'i cuori' as object; literal word order is subject implied -> verb -> object.
segments.paradiso-9-g11: 'drizzando' is a present gerund (drizzare) meaning 'raising/directing/straightening', giving a manner clause: 'drizzando in vanità le vostre tempie' = 'raising your temples in vanity.' Word order has the gerundial phrase after the clause it modifies; 'le vostre tempie' is the direct object.
segments.paradiso-9-g12: 'Ed ecco' is a common poetic particle ('and behold'); 'un altro di quelli splendori' is a noun phrase 'another of those splendors'. 'ver' me' is an archaic contraction of 'verso me' (toward me) and 'si fece' (reflexive past of 'fare') here means 'made itself' or idiomatically 'appeared/approached'.
segments.paradiso-9-g13: 'e ’l suo voler piacermi' = 'and its will/desire to please me' (the enclitic in 'piacermi' marks the person pleased). 'significava' is imperfect 'was signifying/expressed'. 'nel chiarir di fori' is a locative phrase 'in the brightening/clearing of openings' — 'chiarir' (a poetic infinitive/noun) meaning illumination and 'fori' = 'openings/apertures'.
segments.paradiso-9-g54: "Troppo" is an adverb modifying the predicative expression; "sarebbe" is the conditional of essere ('would be'), giving a hypothetical tone; "larga" is an adjective (feminine singular) agreeing with "la bigoncia" which is the subject in post-verbal position ('the tub'); "che" introduces a relative clause and "ricevesse" is an imperfect subjunctive here used to describe the hypothetical content of that tub ('that would receive'); "il sangue ferrarese" is the direct object, literally 'the Ferraran blood' (i.e. blood from Ferrara or bloodshed there).
segments.paradiso-9-g55: "e" links clauses ('and'); "stanco" is a predicate adjective meaning 'tired'; "chi" is an indefinite relative 'he who' (or 'whoever'); "l'" (written here as "’l") stands for the direct object 'it' (the tub's contents/blood); "pesasse" is the imperfect subjunctive/conditional-like form of pesare ('weigh'), used with the hypothetical tone already set; "a oncia a oncia" is an idiom 'ounce by ounce' with the preposition "a" repeated for each measure.
segments.paradiso-9-g56: "che" here links back to the preceding idea ('which/and so'); "donerà" is future indicative of donare ('will give'); "questo prete cortese" is the agent ('this courteous priest'); "per" + infinitive "mostrarsi" gives purpose 'in order to show himself'; "di parte" means 'on a side' i.e. showing partiality/partisanship ("parte" = side).
segments.paradiso-9-g57: "e" continues the sentence ('and'); "cotai" is an archaic plural adjective 'such' (those kinds of); "doni" in the printed word means 'gifts' here, though the provided senses list seems to reflect a different lemma — the sense in context is 'gifts'; "conformi" is predicate adjective 'fitting' or 'in keeping with'; "fieno" is an archaic/poetic form here functioning as the copula 'sono' (meaning 'are') in this line (not literally 'hay'); "al viver del paese" means 'to the way of life of the country/region' ("viver" = mode of living).
segments.paradiso-9-g58: "Sù" is an adverb meaning 'up' or 'above'; "sono" is the present indicative 'are'; "specchi" is the plural noun 'mirrors' acting as the subject that follows the verb (inverted order for poetic effect).
segments.paradiso-9-g59: "voi" is the second-person plural pronoun 'you'; "dicete" is present indicative 2nd plural 'you say' or 'you are called' in this kind of nominative label; "Troni" addresses/identifies them as 'Thrones' (either vocative or nominative used as a name for that order).
segments.paradiso-9-g60: "onde" is a relative adverb 'from which' or 'whence'; "refulge" is present 'shines, beams forth'; "a noi" means 'to us' or 'upon us'; "Dio giudicante" is a noun phrase 'God the Judge' with "giudicante" a present participle used adjectivally ('judging'/'the judging one').
segments.paradiso-9-g61: "sì che" introduces a result clause 'so that'; "questi parlar" = 'these words/speeches' ("parlar" is a noun here); "ne" is a particle with anaphoric force ('in this matter' / 'of it'); "paion" is present indicative 'they seem'; "buoni" is predicate adjective 'good' or 'fitting'.
segments.paradiso-9-g62: "Qui" = 'here' (place adverb). "si tacette" is passato remoto, 3rd singular of tacere with the reflexive/impersonal "si"; together it means 'he/it fell silent' or 'he became silent' (the subject is the person just speaking).
segments.paradiso-9-g63: "e" = 'and'. "fecemi" represents fece + mi (passato remoto of fare + dative pronoun), literally 'he made to me'; idiomatically 'he made (a sign) to me'. "sembiante" is a noun meaning 'appearance/gesture'. The clause introduced by "che" uses the imperfect subjunctive "fosse" (fosse = essere in imperfetto congiuntivo) to report the content of the gesture as hypothetical or indirect ('as if he were/ had turned'). "ad altro volta" = 'to another turn/subject' ("volta" = 'turn' here).
segments.paradiso-9-g64: "per" here indicates movement through/by/toward ('toward' the wheel). "la rota" = 'the wheel'. "in che" = 'in which' (relative pronoun "che"). "si mise" = mise (passato remoto of mettere) with reflexive "si": 'he put/placed himself' or 'took his place'. "com' era davante" = contracted "come era davante," 'as he was before/previously'; "era" is imperfect indicative showing prior state.
segments.paradiso-9-g65: "L’altra letizia" = 'the other joy' ('L’' = the, "altra" = other, "letizia" = joy/delight). This is the subject phrase that the following relative clause qualifies.
segments.paradiso-9-g66: Relative clause introduced by "che" ('which'). "m’era" = mi + era (imperfect of essere) = 'was to me' or 'was known/familiar to me'. "già" = 'already'. "nota" here functions as a past participle/adjective 'known' or 'familiar' (the provided sense list doesn't include 'known', so sense is marked -1). "per cara cosa" = 'as/a dear thing' ("per" = 'for/as', "cara" = 'dear', "cosa" = 'thing').
segments.paradiso-9-g67: "mi si fece in vista" is an impersonal/reflexive construction: literal 'it made itself to me in view' = 'it appeared to me' or 'presented itself to my sight' ("mi" dative, "si fece" passato remoto of farsi). "qual" = 'as'/'such as'. "fin balasso" = an adjectival phrase modifying the comparison: "fin" here means 'fine/delicate' (archaic) and "balasso" = 'buffoon'/'mockery' (so 'a delicate buffoon' or 'a thin/dainty jester'). "in che lo sol percuota" = 'in which the sun strikes it' ("in che" = 'in which', "lo" = 'it/him' referring to the buffoon, "sol" = 'sun' in the text though the supplied sense list does not include this meaning, hence -1, and "percuota" = present subjunctive/indicative form here used as a relative clause verb 'strikes').
segments.paradiso-9-g101: 'Non però qui si pente' is a negative clause with 'non' + adversative 'però' (= 'however'); 'qui' = 'here/at this place'; 'si pente' is an impersonal/reflexive construction (si + third-person sing.) meaning 'one repents' or 'they repent'. Word order (adverbial 'qui' after 'però') is poetic but normal in older Italian.
segments.paradiso-9-g102: 'ma si ride' contrasts with the previous clause: 'ma' = 'but'; 'si ride' again uses the impersonal 'si' with the verb 'ridere' in the third-person singular present, meaning 'one laughs' / 'they laugh'. The clause is short because Dante uses the impersonal to speak of the souls collectively.
segments.paradiso-9-g103: 'non de la colpa' continues the negation begun earlier: 'non' = 'not'; 'de' is an archaic/poetic form of 'di' meaning 'of/about'; 'la colpa' = 'the fault/sin' — the object from which they do not repent. The phrase is elliptical (omits a verb because it is supplied by the context).
segments.paradiso-9-g104: 'ch’a mente non torna' is a relative clause introduced by 'che' (= 'which'); the idiom 'tornare a mente' means 'to come back to mind/occur to memory'. Here 'a' marks the indirect object ('to the mind'), 'mente' = 'memory/mind', and 'torna' is present indicative third-person singular ('does not return/occur').
segments.paradiso-9-g105: 'ma del valor ch’ordinò e provide' resumes the contrast: 'ma' = 'but'; 'del' = di + il = 'of the'; 'valor' here is the moral/powerful quality (Dante's 'valor'); the relative clause 'ch’ordinò e provide' modifies 'valor'. 'ordinò' is passato remoto (3rd sg.) 'ordained/ordered', while 'provide' is an older present form (3rd sg.) meaning 'provides' or 'provides for' — Dante sometimes coordinates verbs of different tenses for rhetorical effect.
segments.paradiso-9-g106: 'Qui si rimira ne l’arte ch’addorna cotanto affetto' — 'Qui si rimira' uses impersonal 'si' + present 'rimirare' meaning 'one contemplates/admires here'. 'ne' = archaic 'in' (in the), so 'ne l'arte' = 'in the art'; the relative clause 'ch’ addorna cotanto affetto' = 'that adorns/ornaments so great an affection'. Word order is somewhat inverted: the direct object 'cotanto affetto' follows the verb for emphasis.
segments.paradiso-9-g107: 'e discernesi ’l bene per che ’l mondo di sù quel di giù torna' — 'discernesi' is a reflexive/passive formation (si + verb) meaning 'is discerned/seen'; '’l bene' = 'the good' is the thing perceived. 'per che' here = 'by/through which' (introducing the cause or rule), and the clause '’l mondo di sù quel di giù torna' states the relation between the upper and lower worlds: 'the world above turns/returns to that below' — 'sù' and 'giù' are adverbs 'up/above' and 'down/below'; 'torna' is present indicative 3rd sg. The whole clause expresses the rule or good by which the cosmic order relates.
segments.paradiso-9-g108: 'Ma perché tutte le tue voglie piene ten porti che son nate in questa spera' — 'Ma' = 'but'; 'perché' = 'because/since'; 'tutte le tue voglie piene' = 'all your desires, full'; 'ten porti' is a contracted old cluster for 'te n' porti' (te = to you, ne = of them) + 'porti' (you carry) and so means 'you carry with you' (second-person present). The relative clause 'che son nate in questa spera' ('that were born in this sphere') uses 'son' = present plural of 'essere' and 'nate' past participle; 'spera' is the older spelling of 'sfera' (sphere).
segments.paradiso-9-g109: 'proceder ancor oltre mi convene.' — infinitive 'proceder' ('procedere') placed at the start for emphasis means 'to proceed'; 'ancor' = 'ancora' = 'still/yet'; 'oltre' = 'further/beyond'; 'mi' is the dative 'to me/for me'; 'convene' is the 3rd singular present of 'convenire' meaning 'it is fitting/necessary' — together 'It is fitting for me to proceed still further.' Note the impersonal construction where the infinitive is the action required of the speaker.
segments.paradiso-9-g110: This whole line is a single interrogative: 'Tu vuo’ saper' = 'Do you want to know' ('vuo":
segments.paradiso-9-g32: The sentence opens with the prepositional phrase D'una radice ('of/from one root') fronted for emphasis. Nacqui is the first-person passato remoto ('I was born'); Dante places the verb before the explicit subjects 'io ed ella' as an afterthought (a poetic inversion), so the literal order is 'From one root I was born — I and she.'
segments.paradiso-9-g33: Fui chiamata is a perfect passive construction using the passato remoto of essere (fui) with the past participle chiamata: 'I was called/named.' The proper name Cunizza stands before the verb for identification.
segments.paradiso-9-g34: Refulgo is present indicative 'I shine/gleam.' Perché introduces a causal clause ('because') with mi vinse (passato remoto of vincere) meaning 'overcame me' where mi is the object. Il lume d'esta stella is the noun phrase 'the light of this star' giving the cause of her shining.
segments.paradiso-9-g35: Indulgo is present indicative 'I indulge' with a dative/emphatic reflexive a me medesma ('to myself, myself indeed'). La cagion di mia sorte is the direct object ('the cause/reason of my fate'). The word order places the object after the verb for a calmer, confessional tone.
segments.paradiso-9-g36: This is a short negative clause: non + verb. Mi is the indirect/object pronoun and noia is the third-person singular present of noiare used impersonally ('it bores/annoys me' or 'it does not trouble me'), so 'and it does not annoy me.'
segments.paradiso-9-g37: Che introduces an explanatory/result clause ('for/that'), and parria is the conditional/imperfect conditional form of parere in Dante's usage meaning 'would seem' or 'might appear.' Forte here means 'harsh/bold' (adjectival/adverbial force), and al vostro vulgo = 'to your common people' (vulgo = the populace). The clause says the admission might seem presumptuous to ordinary listeners.
segments.paradiso-9-g38: Di questa ... gioia is a genitive prepositional phrase 'Of this ... joy.' Luculenta (sumptuous, shining) and cara (dear) are attributive adjectives. Del nostro cielo is a further genitive ('of our heaven'), and the relative clause che più m'è propinqua uses the clitic m' + è ('is to me') to mean 'which is nearest/closest to me' (più + propinqua).
segments.paradiso-9-g39: Simple main clause: grande fama rimase — rimase is passato remoto (or perfect past) of rimanere, meaning 'great fame remained' or 'great renown endured.' The adjective grande modifies fama.
segments.paradiso-9-g40: Pria che is a temporal conjunction meaning 'before.' Moia is Dante's (poetic) form corresponding to muoia/'dies' and here takes the subjunctive after a temporal conjunction: 'before it dies.' The verb refers back to the preceding 'joy' or its fame.
segments.paradiso-9-g41: Quest o centesimo anno is the temporal subject phrase 'this hundredth year.' Ancor 'still' modifies the following reflexive verb s'incinqua, where s' is the reflexive clitic and incinqua (from incinquare/incinquare) means 'is defiled/soiled' or 'becomes sullied' — a present/state sense: 'this hundredth year is still being stained/tainted.'
segments.paradiso-9-g42: Vedi is the imperative/second-person singular 'see' or 'consider.' Se introduces an indirect question 'whether.' Far si dee is an impersonal/modal construction: far (infinitive) + si (impersonal particle 'one') + dee (archaic form of dovere 'ought to/must'), so 'see whether an excellent man ought to do (this).' L'omo eccellente is the logical agent.
segments.paradiso-9-g43: Sì ch' is a result/consequence connector 'so that' (sì che). Altra vita la prima relinqua uses an inverted order: 'another life' (altra vita) is the outcome and 'la prima relinqua' ('he leaves the first behind') uses relinqua (present of relinquere) with la prima ('the first [life]') as object; the implied subject is 'l'omo' from the previous clause.
segments.paradiso-9-g44: The main clause 'E ciò non pensa la turba presente' = 'And the crowd present does not think this.' The subordinate relative 'che Tagliamento e Adice richiude' places the rivers as subject of richiude (present indicative), meaning they enclose/contain — richiude is used in a geographic, enclosing sense.
segments.paradiso-9-g45: Né ... si pente: 'nor ... does it repent' — per esser battuta uses the past participle battuta ('having been beaten') with esser (to be) to explain the cause; si pente is reflexive 'repent/feel remorse.' Ancor = 'still/yet' modifies the temporal sense.
segments.paradiso-9-g46: Ma tosto fia che ... is a future-prospective construction: fia (future of essere) + che introduces the clause 'Padova ... cangerà l’acqua' (Padua will change/redirect the water). Cangerà is future of cangare 'to change/alter.' 'Al palude' expresses destination/tendency toward the marsh.
segments.paradiso-9-g47: 'Per essere al dover le genti crude' is an infinitival/causal phrase: per essere al dover = 'in order to/so as to bring to duty' (duty = dover). 'Le genti crude'—crude is an older adjective meaning harsh/cruel applied to the peoples: the phrase gives the purpose or result of redirecting the waters.
segments.paradiso-9-g48: Dove Sile e Cagnan s’accompagna = 'where the Sile and the Cagnan accompany/join themselves.' The reflexive si + accompagna describes the rivers meeting; Cagnan here is a toponym (small stream). The verb appears singular in form as a stylistic/idiomatic usage describing their junction.
segments.paradiso-9-g49: Tal signoreggia e va con la testa alta: 'Such (a one) rules/dominate and goes with his head high.' Signoreggia is present indicative 'dominates/holds sway'; va con la testa alta is an idiomatic description 'moves/acts with head held high.'
segments.paradiso-9-g50: Che già per lui carpir si fa la ragna is a result clause: che introduces the consequence. Carpir (infinitive) with si fa (reflexive + fare) yields a periphrastic construction 'makes itself ready to seize' — 'la ragna' (the spider) is the agent preparing to snatch; già 'already' temporalizes it.
segments.paradiso-9-g51: Piangerà is future 'will lament' (narrative future) with Feltro (the town) as subject. 'La difalta de l’empio suo pastor' uses difalta (archaic for 'defect/lack/absence') with the genitive de l'empio suo pastor: the town will mourn the absence of its impious shepherd.
segments.paradiso-9-g52: Che sarà sconcia sì: 'who will be so disgraceful/shameful.' Sarà is future of essere; sconcia is an adjective 'shameful/indecent'; sì intensifies 'so' (to the degree that what follows).
segments.paradiso-9-g53: Che per simil non s’entrò in malta: this result clause introduced by che means 'so that such (a thing) did not enter into Malta.' Per simil = 'by/for such (a thing)'; non s’entrò in is past 'did not enter into.' Malta here is used as a proper place-name (island) as a hyperbolic reference.
segments.paradiso-9-g68: Per + noun: 'Per letiziar' uses per = 'by/through' with the noun letiziar (an archaic/poetic plural/noun form meaning 'joys' or 'delights'). 'Là sù' is a two-part locative ('there above'), with sù postpositive. 'Fulgor s’acquista' puts fulgor (subject) before the pronominal verb s’acquista: acquista is 3rd-person singular present of acquistare ('acquires'), and the reflexive/impersonal s’ (si) produces a passive/impersonal sense 'is acquired' or 'one acquires'.
segments.paradiso-9-g69: Sì come = 'just as' (sì = 'so' / come = 'as'). 'Riso' here means a smile or laughter as a visible sign ('a smile'), and 'qui' places it 'here' (in this place/sphere).
segments.paradiso-9-g70: Ma = 'but'; giù = 'down'/'below'. 'S’ abbuia' is s' (si) + abbuia (3rd-sing. present of abbuiare) giving a reflexive/impersonal 'becomes dark/gets dimmed'. 'L’ombra di fuor' is 'the shadow/outward shade' — literal article + noun + di + fuor ('of the outside' = outward appearance).
segments.paradiso-9-g71: Come = 'as' or 'just as'. 'La mente' is the definite phrase 'the mind' (intellect/inner faculty) and è trista uses the present indicative of essere + adjective 'trista' = 'is sad' (a straightforward predicative construction).
segments.paradiso-9-g72: Simple clause: Dio (proper name) is subject, vede is 3rd-person singular present of vedere = 'sees', and tutto is the direct object meaning 'everything'/'all'.
segments.paradiso-9-g73: E = 'and'. 'Tuo veder' uses tuo + the verbal noun/infinitive vedere used as a noun ('your seeing' / 'your vision'). 'S’ inluia' is si + inluia (an archaic/pronominal verb): here it means the vision 'is illumined' or 'is made to gleam' (s' gives a reflexive/impersonal shade).
segments.paradiso-9-g74: Diss' io is the archaic/dialectal past-tense report 'I said' (diss = dissi or 'I said'); the enclitic io reaffirms the speaker 'I'. The apostrophe marks elision common in Dante's orthography.
segments.paradiso-9-g75: Vocative/address: beato spirto = 'blessed spirit' (beato as adjective 'blessed', spirto = archaic form of spirito). The phrase addresses the spirit to whom the speaker speaks.
segments.paradiso-9-g76: Sì che = 'so that'. Nulla = 'nothing'. 'Voglia di sé' = 'a will/desire of itself' (voglia = desire/will); 'a te' = 'to you'. 'Puot’ esser fuia' = archaic forms: puot' is 'può' (can / be able), esser = 'to be', and fuia (from fuggire/fugere) = 'to flee/escape'; together 'may be able to escape'. The whole clause: 'so that nothing of itself can escape you.'
segments.paradiso-9-g77: Dunque = 'then'/'therefore'. 'La voce tua' = 'your voice' (definite article + noun + possessive). The phrase sets up the subject for the following relative clause.
segments.paradiso-9-g78: This long relative clause begins with che = 'which' and modifies 'voce'. '’l ciel' = 'il ciel' (the heaven). 'Trastulla' is 3rd-person singular present of trastullare = 'amuses' or 'delights'. 'Sempre' = 'always/ever'. 'Col canto' = con + il 'with the song'. 'Di quei fuochi pii' = 'of those pious fires/flames' (fuochi = celestial flames, i.e. the angelic lights). The final relative che ... facen la coculla modifies 'fuochi': 'which with six wings make/form the cowl' — facen is an archaic 3rd-plural present (fanno), and la coculla is 'the cowl/hood'.
segments.paradiso-9-g79: Perché = interrogative 'why'. Non satisface = present 'does not satisfy' (satisface = 'satisfy' / 'suffice'). 'A’ miei disii' = 'my desires' (a' = a + i, 'to/for my desires'); note: the lemma/sense provided for disii in the word list does not match Dante's use here ('disii' = 'desires' rather than 'I said'), so sense is marked as unavailable below.
segments.paradiso-9-g80: Già = 'already' or 'by now'. 'Non attendere’ io' shows an archaic contracted ending (attendere' representing an elided form of the first-person inflection); in context it functions as a conditional/irrealis 'I would not wait' or a strong future-like 'I shall not wait'. 'Tua dimanda' = 'your question/request' (dimanda = question/request).
segments.paradiso-9-g81: S’io = 'if I' (s’ is contraction of se). 'M’ intuassi' = m' (to/for myself) + intuassi, an imperfect subjunctive ('if I perceived/sensed/understood') used for a counterfactual condition — 'if I were to perceive/if I perceived'.
segments.paradiso-9-g82: Come = 'how/as'. 'Tu' is the subject 'you', 't’' a reflexive clitic 'yourself', and 'inmii' is an archaic/poetic verb form (2nd-person pronominal form) whose root is rare; in this context it means roughly 'you set/turned yourself' or 'you disposed yourself (toward me)'. Grammatically it is a finite pronominal verb in the clause that 'come' introduces ('how you had directed/affected yourself').
segments.paradiso-9-g124: Per questo is a prepositional phrase meaning 'for this reason' positioned at the start for emphasis. The subject is the coordinated noun phrase l'Evangelio e i dottor magni ('the Gospel and the great doctors'); note the archaic singular-stem form dottor (instead of dottori) followed by the adjective magni. Son is the poetic 3rd-person plural present of essere ('are'), and derelitti is a past participle used predicatively ('abandoned/neglected').
segments.paradiso-9-g125: E solo ai Decretali si studia places the focus on what is studied: 'and only the Decretals are studied.' Solo ('only') restricts the phrase; ai Decretali is a dative/goal ('to/for the Decretals'). Si is the impersonal/passive reflexive used with studia (3rd singular) to mean 'one studies' or 'is studied' — a common medieval/poetic way to express 'people study' or a passive idea.
segments.paradiso-9-g126: Sì che introduces a result clause ('so that'), with pare (3rd-sing. present of parere) meaning 'it seems' or 'it appears.' A' lor vivagni is a small prepositional phrase: a + lor (their) + vivagni (plural noun 'borders/edges/river-banks' used here figuratively). Together the clause expresses the consequence or appearance produced by the preceding fact.
segments.paradiso-9-g127: A questo ('to/with regard to this') is the object of intende. Intende is 3rd-person present of intendere here in the sense 'attends to' or 'gives attention to' (not merely 'intends'); the grammatical subject is the coordinated phrase il papa e ' cardinali ('the pope and the cardinals'). Note Dante's flexible/poetic agreement: intende appears in the singular even though the subject is compound, a usage found in medieval/poetic Italian.
segments.paradiso-9-g128: Non vanno i lor pensieri a Nazarette is a negative declarative: non + vanno (3rd-plural present of andare) = 'do not go.' I lor pensieri ('their thoughts') is the subject (lor is the archaic possessive for loro = 'their'); a Nazarette marks direction or reference ('to Nazareth' / 'to Nazarette'). The whole means their minds are not turned toward that humble place/event.
segments.paradiso-9-g129: Là dove introduces a relative/adverbial clause 'there where.' Gabrïello is the name 'Gabriel' (diaeresis over ï marks the separate syllable in Dante's orthography). Averse l'ali: aperse is the simple past (passato remoto) or poetic past of aprire/aperire meaning 'opened/unfolded,' and l'ali ('the wings') is the direct object. The clause describes the location/event (the Annunciation) that the previous clause says the pope and cardinals do not consider.
segments.paradiso-9-g130: Ma introduces contrast. The coordinated subject Vaticano e l’altre parti elette di Roma ('the Vatican and the other chosen parts/quarters of Rome') is followed by a relative clause che son state cimitero a la milizia che Pietro seguette. Son state is present + past participle (Dante's compound perfect-like construction) meaning 'have been' or 'have served as'; cimitero is literal 'cemetery' and a la milizia means 'for the militia' (i.e. the body of troops/followers). The final che Pietro seguette is a restrictive relative clause with seguette in the past (passato remoto) 'followed' referring to Peter's followers.
segments.paradiso-9-g131: Tosto ('soon/quickly') is an adverb modifying the clause; libere is the adjective 'free' (feminine plural to agree with parti). Fien is an archaic/poetic inflection (here Dante intends a form meaning 'will be' or 'shall be' / 'are to be made free'); this form does not match modern Italian conjugation and is irregular or dialectal. De l' avoltero means 'from the avoltero' (de + l' = 'from the'); avoltero is an obscure, archaic term here (Dante uses an uncommon or regional word) and the precise modern equivalent is uncertain. Overall the clause predicts that those parts of Rome will soon be freed from that thing/person called avoltero.
segments.paradiso-9-g83: "La maggior valle in che l’acqua si spanda" is a relative clause headed by 'La maggior valle' (the superlative adjective 'maggior' before the noun is an apocopated form of 'maggiore'). 'in che' here means 'into/where' and introduces the clause. 'l’acqua si spanda' uses the clitic 'si' with the verb 'spandere' in the present form 'spanda' (poetic spelling) to express a reflexive/impersonal sense: 'the water spreads (itself)/is spread' into that valley.
segments.paradiso-9-g84: The verb-first line 'incominciaro allor le sue parole' places the passato remoto 'incominciaro' (archaic/poetic form of 'incominciarono') before its subject for emphasis: 'then his words began'. 'allor' is an adverb meaning 'then', and 'le sue parole' is the delayed subject 'his words/speech'.
segments.paradiso-9-g85: 'fuor di' is an archaic compound meaning 'outside of'. 'quel mar' = 'that sea' and the following relative clause 'che la terra inghirlanda' uses the present 'inghirlanda' (verb form) meaning 'crowns/garlands' — i.e. 'the sea that garlands the land'. Word order is standard for a relative clause.
segments.paradiso-9-g86: The prepositional phrase 'tra discordanti liti' = 'between discordant/contending shores/quarrels' (poetic image). 'contra ’l sole' means 'toward/against the sun' (''l' = il). 'tanto sen va' contains the adverb 'tanto' ('so much') and the contracted pronominal cluster 'sen' (= se + ne) with the present 'va' of 'andare': literally 'it goes away/extends so far'.
segments.paradiso-9-g87: The clause beginning with 'che' ('so that/which') has 'fa' (3rd sing. of 'fare') functioning as a catenative/causative verb 'makes/turns into'. 'meridïano' is used as a predicate complement ('meridian'/'southing'); 'là dove' = 'there where'. The final phrase 'pria far suole' is archaic inversion: 'pria' = 'formerly/before', and 'suole' (from 'solere') with infinitive 'far' expresses habitual action ('used to make/do'), so literally 'where the horizon formerly used to do so.'
segments.paradiso-9-g88: 'Di quella valle' ('of/from that valley') is the locative/genitive phrase. 'fu’ io' is the passato remoto 'fu' (was) with the subject pronoun 'io' placed after the verb for emphasis: 'I was'. 'litorano' is predicative (a coastal/shore person). 'tra Ebro e Macra' is a simple 'between X and Y' phrase naming the two rivers/places.
segments.paradiso-9-g89: The relative 'che' introduces a clause modifying the place just named. 'per cammin corto' means 'by a short walking/path' ('per' + noun expresses manner/distance). 'parte' here functions as a verb meaning 'separates/part(s)' (poetic usage), and the construction 'parte lo Genovese dal Toscano' means 'divides the Genoese from the Tuscan' (lo/dal = articles contracted).
segments.paradiso-9-g90: 'Ad un occaso quasi e ad un orto' is a double locative: 'at a kind of sunset and at a garden'. 'Buggea siede' places the proper name before the verb 'siede' (3rd sing. present of 'siedere') meaning 'is situated/sits'. 'e la terra ond’ io fui' links a second subject 'and the land whence I was' — 'ond’' is a contracted form of 'onde' meaning 'from which/whence', and 'io fui' is the past 'I was' (inversion after the relative).
segments.paradiso-9-g91: The relative 'che' ('which') begins the clause modifying 'la terra'. 'fé' (archaic/poetic past of 'fece') is a past tense of 'fare' meaning 'made/caused'. Word order is inverted for emphasis: 'ché fé del sangue suo già caldo il porto' = 'which made the harbor warm with its blood' (note 'del sangue suo' = 'of its/his blood', 'già' here 'formerly/once', and 'il porto' appears after the verb as the thing affected).
segments.paradiso-9-g92: Subject 'Folco' + verb 'mi disse' = 'Folco said/told me'. 'mi' is the indirect object clitic 'to me'. 'quella gente a cui fu noto il nome mio' is a subordinate relative: 'that people to whom my name was known' — 'a cui' = 'to whom', and 'fu noto' is passive/perfect in passato remoto: 'was known'.
segments.paradiso-9-g93: 'e questo cielo di me s’imprenta' links the clause with 'and'. 'questo cielo' is the subject. 'di me s’imprenta' uses the reflexive/passive clitic 's’' with 'imprenta' (poetic form of 'imprente'/'imprenta') meaning 'is imprinted by/it imprints itself on me' — here the phrasing means 'this heaven stamps/imprints me'.
segments.paradiso-9-g94: 'com’ io fe’ di lui' is a short comparative clause: 'as I did of him' or 'just as I impressed him'. 'com’' = 'come/as', and 'fe’' is a reduced/poetic form related to 'feci' (I did/made) — here the lexeme listed as 'fe' in the word list does not correspond to its ordinary senses, so the precise verbal sense is the past first-person 'I made/did'.
segments.paradiso-9-g95: 'ché più non arse la figlia di Belo' begins an explanatory clause with 'ché' = 'for/since'. 'più non' = 'no longer'. 'arse' (past of 'arere/ardere') = 'burned' (i.e. burned with passion). The syntax is subject 'la figlia di Belo' after the verb (poetic inversion): 'for the daughter of Belo no longer burned.'
segments.paradiso-9-g96: The participle/gerund 'noiando' (from 'noiare') plus 'e a Sicheo e a Creusa' lists those she tormented/affected: 'noiando e a Sicheo e a Creusa' = 'causing trouble/annoyance (or courting) both Sychaeus and Creusa'. Here 'a' marks the people affected. The construction attaches back to the previous clause (she no longer burned for me, tormenting those people).
segments.paradiso-9-g97: The simple prepositional phrase 'di me' = 'about me/of me' stands alone here as the object of the preceding gerund/participle ('noiando di me' = 'making trouble about me').
segments.paradiso-9-g98: 'infin che si convenne al pelo' = 'until/until it came to the edge/point'. 'infin che' = 'until', 'si convenne' is passato remoto of 'convenire' with reflexive/clitic 'si' meaning 'it came together/it was fitting/it came to pass', and 'al pelo' ('to the hair/edge') is an idiom meaning 'to the very point/edge'.
segments.paradiso-9-g99: The negative 'né' begins a further negation: 'nor that Rhodopean (woman) who was deceived by Demofoonte'. 'delusa' is a past participle meaning 'deceived'/'disappointed' and 'fu da Demofoonte' = 'was by Demofoonte' (passive voice).
segments.paradiso-9-g100: 'né Alcide quando Iole nel core ebbe rinchiusa' continues the negative list: 'nor Alcides when he had enclosed Iole in his heart'. 'quando' introduces the temporal clause, 'nel core' = 'in the heart', and 'ebbe rinchiusa' is passato remoto of 'avere' + past participle 'rinchiusa' = 'had shut/enclosed'.
segments.paradiso-9-g111: Or sappi is an imperative address: Or = 'now' and sappi is 2nd-person singular imperative of sapere ('know'). che introduces a subordinate clause. Là entro is a two-word locative ('there within'), and si tranquilla places the reflexive clitic si before the verb to form a middle/reflexive expression meaning 'is tranquil' (poetic word order puts the name Raab after the predicate).
segments.paradiso-9-g112: e introduces a coordinated clause. a + nostr' ordine is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to/into our order' (nostr' is an elided/trochaic contraction of nostro). congiunta is a feminine past participle/adjective ('joined') agreeing with Raab.
segments.paradiso-9-g113: di lei ('concerning her' or 'of her') is an object/agent phrase placed before the rest for emphasis. nel sommo grado is an adverbial phrase 'in the highest rank/degree'. si sigilla is the present indicative of sigillare with the reflexive/passive si, here meaning 'is sealed' or 'is fixed/confirmed' (third person singular), so the clause reads literally 'of her in the highest degree is sealed.'
segments.paradiso-9-g114: Da questo cielo is a simple prepositional phrase meaning 'from this heaven' or 'out of this sphere'; Da marks source and questo is the proximal demonstrative 'this'.
segments.paradiso-9-g115: in cui is a relative pronoun 'in which' referring back to 'questo cielo'. l'ombra s'appunta uses the clitic s' (reflexive/mediopassive) with appunta (present) to mean 'the shadow is focused/comes to a point'; che introduces a further clause modifying 'ombra' — che 'l vostro mondo face' = 'that your world makes/fashions' (face = archaic/metrical form of fa/facere).
segments.paradiso-9-g116: pria ch' = 'before that' (pria = before; ch' = che). altr' alma = 'any other soul' (altr' = altro, elided). fu assunta is a compound past passive: fu (3rd sg. of essere in passato) + assunta (past participle) = 'was taken up/was assumed' (passive perfect), with agreement between alma (fem.) and assunta.
segments.paradiso-9-g117: Ben si convenne is an impersonal idiom: Ben ('indeed/well') + si convenne (passive/impersonal past 'it was fitting/it was right'). lei lasciar uses the direct object lei with the infinitive lasciar as the logical complement of convenne ('that she be left/assigned'). per palma = 'as a palm' (palma here = symbol of victory), in alcun cielo = 'in some heaven', de l'alta vittoria = 'of the high/lofty victory'. che s'acquistò is a relative clause 'which she acquired/won' where s' is reflexive/middile and acquistò is passato remoto 3rd sg.
segments.paradiso-9-g118: perch' ella favorò = 'because she favored' (perch' = perché 'because', favorò = passato remoto 'she granted/assisted'). la prima gloria di Iosüè is the direct object ('the first glory of Joshua'); in su la Terra Santa is a prepositional phrase ('upon the Holy Land') where in su is the slightly redundant preposition cluster used in poetry.
segments.paradiso-9-g119: che introduces a relative clause 'which ...'. poco is an adverb 'little' modifying tocca (present indicative of toccare), where tocca here means 'matters to' or 'concerns'. The phrase al papa la memoria uses inverted word order for emphasis: 'the memory [of it] matters little to the pope' (i.e. the pope scarcely remembers/cares).
segments.paradiso-9-g120: La tua città is a simple noun phrase 'your city' (La definite article + possessive tua + città). The phrase sets the subject for the following relative clauses.
segments.paradiso-9-g121: che introduces a relative clause: 'which of him is pianta' uses pianta ('plant'/'offspring') metaphorically to mean 'descended from/sprung from him'. che pria volse le spalle al suo fattore = 'who first turned his back on his Maker' (pria = before/first; volse is passato remoto of volgere 'turned'; le spalle al suo fattore = 'his back to his Maker'). e di cui è la nvidia tanto pianta = 'and whose envy is so deeply planted' (di cui = 'of which/of whom', la nvidia = 'the envy', pianta used metaphorically as 'rootedness').
segments.paradiso-9-g122: produce e spande are present indicative verbs 'it produces and scatters/spreads'. il maladetto fiore is the subject 'the accursed flower'. c' ha disvïate is a contracted colloquial/poetic form for che ha disvïate ('which has led astray'); disvïate is a past participle 'led astray' agreeing with its objects le pecore e li agni ('the sheep and the lambs'). Note c' here is a contraction (che/ci) in the text; in meaning it functions as the relative 'which' and is contracted in speech.
segments.paradiso-9-g123: però che here introduces the causal clause 'because' (però che = 'therefore/because that'). fatto ha is literal 'has made' (fatto = past participle used with avere to mean 'made/made to be'), and lupo del pastore is a predicative phrase 'a wolf of the shepherd' (meaning the shepherd has been turned into a wolf). The construction is causative/resultive: 'it has made the shepherd a wolf.'

sentences.paradiso-9-s5: The eyes of Beatrice, which were fixed above me, as before, confirmed by their dear assent to my desire, remained steady.
sentences.paradiso-9-s6: "Ah, grant at once a recompense to my will, blessed spirit," I said, "and let me have proof that I may in you reflect that which I think!"
sentences.paradiso-9-s8: So the light that was still new to me, from its depths — whence it had first sung — continued, like to one for whom doing good is beneficial: "In that part of perverse Italy that lies between Rialto and the springs of the Brenta and the Piave a hill rises, and it does not rise very high; therefrom already descended a torch that made a great assault upon the district."
sentences.paradiso-9-s1: Since your Charles, fair Clemency, had made me clear, he told me the deceits that his seed was to receive; but he said, "Be silent and let the years move"; so that I can say nothing except that a righteous lament will come after your losses.
sentences.paradiso-9-s2: And already the life of that holy light had turned toward the Sun that fills it, like that good which is so great for every thing.
sentences.paradiso-9-s3: Ah, deluded souls and impious devices, who from such a good twist your hearts, raising your temples in vanity!
sentences.paradiso-9-s4: And behold another of those splendors came toward me, and its wish to please me was signified in the brightening of openings.
sentences.paradiso-9-s13: The tub that would take in Ferrara's blood would be far too large, and he who weighed it ounce by ounce would grow weary; this courteous priest will give such things to show himself partisan, and such gifts are fitting to the life of the country.
sentences.paradiso-9-s14: Up there are mirrors; you are called Thrones; from them God the Judge shines upon us; so that these words seem good to us.
sentences.paradiso-9-s15: Here he fell silent; and he made a gesture to me as if he had turned to another matter, toward the wheel in which he set himself as he had been before.
sentences.paradiso-9-s16: The other joy, which had already been familiar to me as a dear thing, presented itself to my sight like a delicate buffoon whom the sun strikes.
sentences.paradiso-9-s25: Here they do not repent, however; they laugh — not at the sin, which does not come back to mind, but at the valor that ordains and provides.
sentences.paradiso-9-s26: Here one contemplates the art that so adorns such affection, and the good is discerned by which the world above returns to that below.
sentences.paradiso-9-s27: But because you carry with you all your full desires that were born in this sphere, I must go on further still.
sentences.paradiso-9-s28: Do you want to know who is in this little light that sparkles so near me, like a ray of sun in clear water?
sentences.paradiso-9-s9: She and I were born of one root. I was called Cunizza, and here I shine because the light of this star overcame me; but gladly I indulge to myself the cause of my fate, and it does not bother me; for it might perhaps seem bold to your common people.
sentences.paradiso-9-s10: Of this bright and dear joy of our heaven that is nearest to me, great fame remained; and before it dies, this hundredth year is still defiled: consider whether an excellent man ought to act thus, so that he leaves his first life for another.
sentences.paradiso-9-s11: And the crowd present does not think this — that the Tagliamento and the Adige enclose it — nor does it yet repent for having been beaten; but soon it will be that Padua will turn the water that Vicenza waters toward the marsh, to bring the cruel peoples to duty; and where the Sile and the Cagnan meet, he so rules and walks with his head held high that already the spider makes itself ready to seize (on his account).
sentences.paradiso-9-s12: Feltre will still lament the absence of its impious shepherd, who will be so disgraced that the like had not entered Malta.
sentences.paradiso-9-s17: By joy up there one acquires a radiance, just as here one acquires a smile; but down below the outward shadow is darkened, as the mind is sad.
sentences.paradiso-9-s18: "God sees all, and your seeing is illumined (is made to gleam)," I said, "blessed spirit, so that nothing of itself may be able to escape you."
sentences.paradiso-9-s19: Then your voice — which amuses heaven forever with the song of those pious flames that, with six wings, make (form) the cowl — why does it not satisfy my desires?
sentences.paradiso-9-s20: I would no longer wait for your question, if I perceived it myself how you had disposed yourself toward me.
sentences.paradiso-9-s33: Because of this the Gospel and the great doctors are abandoned; only the Decretals are studied, so that it seems as if they cared only for their own borders.
sentences.paradiso-9-s34: The pope and the cardinals attend to this; their thoughts do not go to Nazareth, where Gabriel unfolded his wings.
sentences.paradiso-9-s35: But the Vatican and the other chosen quarters of Rome, which have been a cemetery to the militia that followed Peter, will soon be freed from the avoltero.
sentences.paradiso-9-s21: “The largest valley into which the water spreads,” then his words began, “lies outside that sea which garlands the land; it runs so far between discordant shores facing the sun that it makes a meridian where the horizon used to do so.”
sentences.paradiso-9-s22: From that valley I was a shore-dweller between the Ebro and the Macra, which by a short walk/route separates the Genoese from the Tuscan.
sentences.paradiso-9-s23: At a sort of sunset and at a garden Buggea sits, and the land whence I was; which once made the harbor warm with its blood.
sentences.paradiso-9-s24: Folco told me of those people to whom my name was known; and this heaven imprints me, as I imprinted him; for the daughter of Belo no longer burned (with passion) for me, troubling both Sychaeus and Creusa about me, until she came to the very point; nor (was it) that Rhodopean woman who was deceived by Demofoon, nor Alcides when he had shut Iole up in his heart.
sentences.paradiso-9-s29: Now know that Rahab is tranquil within there; and, joined to our order, she is sealed in the highest degree.
sentences.paradiso-9-s30: From this heaven — in which the shadow that fashions your world is focused — before any other soul was taken up to Christ's triumph.
sentences.paradiso-9-s31: It was fitting that she be left as a palm in some heaven of the lofty victory that she won with both one palm and the other, because she favored Joshua's first glory in the Holy Land, a thing of which the pope has little memory.
sentences.paradiso-9-s32: Your city, which is the offspring of him who first turned his back on his Maker, and whose envy is so deeply planted, produces and spreads the accursed flower that has led the sheep and the lambs astray, because it has made the shepherd a wolf.
